title: Acceleration of Free Fall — PYP Mastery

questions.question: A skydiver falls through the air. Initially, his speed increases. What happens to his acceleration as he falls?
options: It decreases until it becomes zero
It remains constant at $9.8\text{ m/s}^2$
It increases as his speed increases
It remains constant at $0\text{ m/s}^2$
questions.explanation:
Air resistance increases with speed, reducing the resultant force and decreasing acceleration until terminal velocity is reached ($a = 0$). 

**Common mistake:** Confusing speed and acceleration; speed increases while acceleration decreases.

questions.question: The diagram shows a speed–time graph for an object falling in a uniform gravitational field. Which section of the graph shows terminal velocity?
options: The horizontal section
The straight diagonal section starting from the origin
The curved section between the diagonal and horizontal
The area under the whole graph
questions.explanation:
Terminal velocity occurs when the speed is constant, shown as a horizontal line on a speed–time graph. 

**Common mistake:** Selecting the diagonal section, which represents constant acceleration.

questions.question: Which statement describes the motion of an object falling in a vacuum near the Earth's surface?
options: It falls with a constant acceleration of $9.8\text{ m/s}^2$
It falls with a constant speed of $9.8\text{ m/s}$
Its acceleration decreases as its speed increases
Heavier objects fall with greater acceleration than lighter objects
questions.explanation:
In a vacuum, there is no air resistance to decrease acceleration, so it remains constant at $g$. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking terminal velocity applies in a vacuum.

questions.question: A ball is thrown vertically upwards. At its highest point, what are its speed and acceleration? (Take $g = 10\text{ m/s}^2$ and ignore air resistance)
options: Speed: $0\text{ m/s}$; Acceleration: $10\text{ m/s}^2$ downwards
Speed: $0\text{ m/s}$; Acceleration: $0\text{ m/s}^2$
Speed: $10\text{ m/s}$; Acceleration: $10\text{ m/s}^2$ downwards
Speed: $0\text{ m/s}$; Acceleration: $10\text{ m/s}^2$ upwards
questions.explanation:
At the peak, the speed is momentarily zero. However, gravity ($g$) still acts on the ball, providing a constant acceleration of $10\text{ m/s}^2$ downwards. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking acceleration is zero when speed is zero.

questions.question: An object falling through air reaches terminal velocity. Which statement about the forces is correct?
options: The weight is equal to the air resistance
The weight is greater than the air resistance
The air resistance is greater than the weight
There are no forces acting on the object
questions.explanation:
Terminal velocity is reached when the upward force of air resistance balances the downward force of weight, resulting in zero acceleration. 

**Common mistake:** Thinking air resistance must be greater than weight for the speed to stop increasing.
